seo.title: Single-Agent vs Multi-Agent Safety Patterns
seo.description: Compare Single-Agent Architecture and Multi-Agent Architecture for AI agent reliability. Understand trade-offs and when to use each approach.

content.intro: Choosing between single-agent and multi-agent architectures is one of the most consequential decisions in AI system design. Each approach carries distinct tradeoffs in complexity, reliability, and scalability. Understanding these differences helps teams build systems that are both effective and verifiable at runtime.

approachA.name: Single-Agent Architecture
approachA.description: A single-agent architecture relies on one AI model or agent to handle an entire task or workflow end-to-end. This approach keeps the system simple and centralized, with a single point of control and a linear execution path. It is often the right starting point for well-scoped, lower-complexity use cases.
strengths: Simpler to build, debug, and reason about due to a single execution context
Lower latency with no inter-agent communication overhead
Easier to trace and audit a single chain of decisions
Reduced surface area for failure modes and unexpected interactions
weaknesses: Context window limitations constrain how much information the agent can hold at once
A single point of failure means one error can derail the entire task
Difficult to scale to complex, multi-step workflows requiring specialized reasoning
Hard to parallelize work, leading to slower throughput on large tasks

approachB.name: Multi-Agent Architecture
approachB.description: A multi-agent architecture distributes work across several specialized AI agents that collaborate, delegate, and verify each other's outputs. This enables parallelism, specialization, and redundancy, making it well-suited for complex, long-horizon tasks. However, the added coordination introduces new failure modes that require careful oversight.
strengths: Enables specialization, with each agent optimized for a specific subtask or domain
Supports parallel execution, significantly reducing end-to-end task time
Redundancy and cross-checking between agents can improve output quality
More scalable for complex workflows that exceed a single agent's capacity
weaknesses: Significantly more complex to design, coordinate, and debug across agent boundaries
Inter-agent communication introduces latency, cost, and potential for cascading errors
Emergent behaviors from agent interactions are difficult to predict or control
Harder to audit and trace decisions when responsibility is distributed across agents

comparison.approachA: Low — single execution path, straightforward to reason about
comparison.approachB: High — coordination logic, message passing, and emergent behaviors add significant complexity

comparison.approachA: Predictable but brittle — one failure point can break the whole task
comparison.approachB: Resilient by design but prone to subtle, hard-to-detect coordination failures

comparison.approachA: Limited by context window and sequential processing constraints
comparison.approachB: Highly scalable through parallelism and task decomposition

comparison.approachA: Easy to trace — a single decision chain is straightforward to log and audit
comparison.approachB: Challenging — distributed decisions require robust tracing across agent boundaries

comparison.approachA: Simpler to instrument with runtime checks at key decision points
comparison.approachB: Critical and more complex — each agent handoff is a potential failure point requiring verification

recommendation.heading: Which Should You Choose?
recommendation.summary: Start with a single-agent architecture when your task is well-scoped and you prioritize simplicity, auditability, and fast iteration. Adopt a multi-agent architecture when task complexity, scale, or specialization demands it — but invest heavily in observability and runtime verification to manage the coordination risks. In either case, runtime verification is not optional: it is the mechanism that keeps your agents behaving as intended as complexity grows.

cta.heading: Try Runtime Verification with Vex
cta.description: Whether you are running a single agent or orchestrating dozens, Vex gives you the runtime visibility and verification layer you need to catch failures before they reach your users.
